compliance_mappings.notes: Both require maintaining a comprehensive inventory of all accounts with regular review and removal of unauthorized accounts
Both require formal identity management including registration and deregistration of user identities
NIST IA-5(1) is more detailed on password storage and breach-checking; CIS 5.2 focuses on unique passwords across enterprise assets
ISO A.5.17 covers authentication information management broadly; CIS 5.2 specifically requires unique passwords per system
Both require automatic disabling of dormant accounts after a defined inactivity period (CIS specifies 45 days)
ISO A.5.18 covers access rights review broadly; CIS 5.3 specifically targets dormant account identification and disabling
Both require dedicated administrator accounts separate from day-to-day user accounts for privileged access
NIST AC-6(5) covers restricting and registering privileged accounts; CIS 5.4 focuses on the separation aspect
Both require privileged access through dedicated accounts with separation from routine activities
NIST AC-2 covers all account types; CIS 5.5 specifically addresses service account inventory with owner, purpose, and authorization
ISO A.5.16 covers all identity management; CIS 5.5 specifically targets service account identification and ownership documentation
NIST AC-2(1) covers automated account management broadly; CIS 5.6 specifically requires centralized account management through directory services
ISO A.5.16 covers identity management; CIS 5.6 adds the centralization requirement through a single directory or identity provider
NIST AC-1 covers access control policy governance; CIS 6.1 specifically mandates a formal process for granting access with documented requests and approvals
NIST AC-2 covers account management; CIS 6.1 specifically addresses the access granting workflow with request, approval, and notification
ISO A.5.15 defines access control policy; CIS 6.1 operationalizes this with a specific access granting process
Both require formal access provisioning processes with authorization from asset owners and least privilege application
NIST AC-2 covers full account management; CIS 6.2 specifically addresses the access revoking process triggered by HR events
ISO A.5.18 covers access rights modification and removal; CIS 6.2 adds specific timing requirements (24 hours) for revocation
Both address prompt access revocation upon termination or role change with verification of completeness
NIST IA-2(1) mandates MFA for privileged accounts; CIS 6.3 specifically requires MFA for all externally-exposed applications
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication broadly; CIS 6.3 specifically mandates MFA for all externally-exposed applications
NIST AC-17 covers remote access broadly; CIS 6.4 specifically mandates MFA for remote network access with phishing-resistant preference
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; CIS 6.4 specifically requires MFA for remote network access including VPN and RDP
NIST AC-6(5) covers privileged account restriction; CIS 6.5 adds the specific MFA requirement for all administrative access
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access management; CIS 6.5 adds the requirement for phishing-resistant MFA for all admin access
NIST AC-2 covers account management; CIS 6.6 specifically addresses maintaining an inventory of authentication and authorization systems themselves
ISO A.5.9 covers asset inventory broadly; CIS 6.6 specifically targets the inventory of identity infrastructure (IdPs, directories, auth systems)
NIST AC-2(1) covers automated account management; CIS 6.7 specifically mandates centralizing access control through SSO or directory services
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy; CIS 6.7 operationalizes centralized enforcement through a single identity provider
Both require defining and maintaining role-based access control with documented role definitions and least privilege enforcement
NIST AC-6 covers least privilege broadly; CIS 6.8 implements it through RBAC role definitions and annual reviews
ISO A.5.15 mentions access control models; CIS 6.8 specifically mandates documented RBAC with annual role reviews
Both require logical access security mechanisms that enforce approved authorizations using ACLs, RBAC, ABAC
NIST AC-6 is specifically least privilege; SOC 2 CC6.1 covers broader logical access security software and architectures
Both establish access control policy based on business and security requirements with least privilege and need-to-know
Both require technical enforcement of information access restrictions at all access points
Both cover user registration, authorization, provisioning, periodic review, and timely deprovisioning
Both address identity lifecycle management including registration, verification, and removal
Both require formal access provisioning with periodic review and prompt revocation upon role change or termination
NIST AC-5 covers segregation of duties; SOC 2 CC6.3 covers both SoD and role-based least privilege in one control
NIST AC-6 covers least privilege; SOC 2 CC6.3 combines this with role-based access and segregation of duties
NIST AC-3(7) covers RBAC specifically; SOC 2 CC6.3 combines RBAC with least privilege and SoD
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy broadly; SOC 2 CC6.3 specifically operationalizes RBAC and least privilege
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access; SOC 2 CC6.3 covers privileged access restriction as part of broader role-based access
NIST AC-3 covers logical access enforcement; SOC 2 CC6.4 covers physical access restriction with identity integration
Both address physical entry controls with badge-based systems integrated with identity management infrastructure
NIST AC-17 covers remote access; SOC 2 CC6.6 covers broader external threat protection including MFA and boundary protection
NIST AC-17(2) covers remote access encryption; SOC 2 CC6.6 includes TLS 1.2+ requirement as part of external threat protection
ISO A.8.20 covers network security; SOC 2 CC6.6 includes network boundary protection with identity-aware controls
Both address controlling information flow including identity data transmission with encryption and authorization requirements
Both require secure transfer rules including encryption for identity data, SCIM provisioning traffic, and federation assertions
NIST AC-19 covers mobile device access control; SOC 2 CC6.8 covers broader malicious software prevention including credential-theft malware
ISO A.8.1 covers endpoint device protection; SOC 2 CC6.8 specifically addresses malware prevention for credential stores and identity infrastructure
Both require monitoring of identity and access systems for anomalous patterns including impossible travel and privilege escalation
NIST AC-2(4) covers account lifecycle auditing; SOC 2 CC7.2 covers broader real-time security monitoring including identity systems
ISO A.8.15 covers logging; SOC 2 CC7.2 emphasizes real-time monitoring and analysis of security events including identity attacks
Both require monitoring for anomalous behaviour with appropriate actions including identity-related incident detection
NIST AC-20 covers external system access; SOC 2 CC9.2 covers broader third-party identity risk assessment including IAM practices evaluation
Both address supplier identity and access management risk assessment including authentication standards and access governance
Both require contractual security requirements with suppliers including MFA, credential management, and breach notification
Both define password security requirements including minimum length, breach-checking, no forced rotation, and no composition rules
NIST AC-7 covers failed logon limits; OWASP V2.2 covers broader authenticator security including anti-automation and rate limiting
NIST IA-5 covers authenticator management; OWASP V2.2 covers general authenticator security requirements for all auth mechanisms
NIST IA-5(1) covers password-based auth broadly; OWASP V2.4 specifically mandates credential storage algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) with parameters
NIST IA-5(1) addresses password management; OWASP V2.5 specifically covers credential recovery with anti-enumeration and secure token requirements
NIST IA-2(6) covers separate device MFA; OWASP V2.7 covers out-of-band verifier requirements including SMS, push, and voice
NIST IA-5 covers authenticator management broadly; OWASP V2.8 specifically covers TOTP/HOTP OTP requirements with entropy and drift
NIST IA-2(1) mandates MFA; OWASP V2.9 specifically covers FIDO2/WebAuthn cryptographic verifier implementation requirements
NIST IA-5 covers authenticator management; OWASP V2.10 specifically covers service-to-service authentication using mTLS and OAuth client credentials
Both require session authenticity with cryptographic tokens, new session on auth, and secure transmission channels
NIST SC-23 covers session authenticity; OWASP V3.2 adds specific session binding requirements including server-side validation and JWT claims
NIST SC-23(3) covers unique session identifiers; OWASP V3.2 extends to binding sessions to user, auth method, and TLS connection
Both require session termination with idle timeout, absolute timeout, server-side invalidation, and accessible logout
NIST AC-2(5) covers inactivity logout broadly; OWASP V3.3 provides specific idle and absolute timeout maximums
NIST SC-23 covers session authenticity; OWASP V3.5 specifically covers JWT/OAuth token security with algorithm restrictions and rotation
NIST AC-10 covers concurrent sessions; OWASP V3.7 covers broader session exploit defenses including fixation prevention and session visibility
Both require centralized, server-side access control enforcement with deny-by-default and least privilege
NIST AC-25 is the theoretical reference monitor; OWASP V4.1 provides practical implementation guidance for centralized access control
Both require technical enforcement of access restrictions at all access points including APIs and databases
NIST AC-3 covers access enforcement broadly; OWASP V4.2 specifically addresses operation-level access control including IDOR prevention
NIST AC-6 covers least privilege; OWASP V4.2 covers preventing both vertical and horizontal privilege escalation at the API level
NIST AC-3 covers access enforcement; OWASP V4.3 covers additional access control considerations including CSRF and bulk operation authorization
NIST AC-6(5) restricts privileged accounts; OWASP V4.3 requires MFA and network restrictions for administrative interfaces
Traditional ZT maturity maps to basic NIST AC-3 enforcement with static password-based authentication at the perimeter
Initial ZT maturity requires MFA for privileged and external users, mapping to NIST IA-2(1) MFA for privileged accounts
Initial ZT maturity requires centralized IdP, mapping to NIST AC-2(1) automated account management through centralized directory
Advanced ZT maturity requires continuous risk evaluation, mapping to NIST AC-2(12) account monitoring for atypical usage
Advanced ZT maturity continuous authorization maps to NIST AC-3(13) attribute-based access control with dynamic context evaluation
Optimal ZT maturity with universal enforcement maps to NIST AC-25 reference monitor concepts for complete access mediation
CISA MFA progression from basic to phishing-resistant directly maps to NIST IA-2(1) MFA requirement with increasing assurance
Both require automated identity lifecycle management integrated with HR systems for provisioning and deprovisioning
NIST AC-2(2) covers temporary account removal; CISA lifecycle automation covers broader automated lifecycle including JML processes
NIST AC-6(5) restricts privileged accounts; CISA PAM maturity extends to JIT access, session recording, and zero standing privilege
NIST AC-17(4) covers remote privileged access; CISA PAM maturity includes PAWs, session recording, and AI-driven anomaly detection
AAL1 single-factor authentication satisfies the basic requirement of NIST IA-2 unique identification and authentication
Both require multi-factor authentication; AAL2 defines the specific assurance level that maps to NIST IA-2(1) MFA requirements
Both require hardware cryptographic authentication with verifier impersonation resistance on a separate device
Both require government-issued photo ID verification for identity proofing at the moderate assurance level
Both require in-person identity evidence validation and verification at the highest assurance level
NIST IA-8 covers non-organizational user authentication; FAL1 defines the basic federation assurance level for identity assertions
NIST AC-1 covers access control policy; SOC 2 CC5.2 specifically covers risk assessment of identity and access controls
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy; SOC 2 CC5.2 specifically addresses risk assessment informing control selection
NIST AC-3 covers access enforcement; SOC 2 CC8.1 covers change management for identity system configurations
Both require configuration management for identity systems with security review, testing, and rollback capability
NIST AC-1 covers management commitment; SOC 2 CC1.4 specifically requires board-level oversight with IAM metrics reporting
ISO A.5.1 covers management-approved security policies; SOC 2 CC1.4 requires board-level oversight and IAM KPI reporting
NIST AC-5 covers segregation of duties; SOC 2 CC3.2 addresses identity fraud risk assessment including collusion and social engineering
ISO A.5.3 covers segregation of duties; SOC 2 CC3.2 specifically addresses fraud risk in identity governance processes
ISO A.5.17 covers authentication information management broadly; OWASP V2.1 provides specific password length, composition, and breach-checking requirements
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; OWASP V2.2 adds specific anti-automation, rate limiting, and error response requirements
ISO A.8.24 covers cryptography use; OWASP V2.4 specifies exact credential storage algorithms (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt) with minimum parameters
ISO A.8.5 mentions MFA; OWASP V2.9 provides specific FIDO2/WebAuthn implementation requirements for cryptographic verifiers
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication broadly; OWASP V3.3 provides specific session timeout maximums (30 min idle, 12 hr absolute)
Both require operation-level access restriction enforcement at all access points including API endpoints
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; CISA initial maturity specifically requires centralized IdP and phishing-resistant MFA deployment
CISA advanced maturity requires full lifecycle automation with SCIM; ISO A.5.18 covers access rights review but allows manual processes
CISA visibility covers all identity event logging and UEBA analytics; NIST AC-2(4) is limited to account lifecycle auditing
CISA visibility requires AI/ML-driven analytics at higher maturity levels; ISO A.8.15 covers standard logging requirements
CISA automation covers self-healing identity infrastructure at optimal; NIST AC-2(1) covers basic automated account management
NIST AC-1 covers access control policy; CISA governance covers zero trust steering committees, KPIs, and identity-specific roadmaps
ISO A.5.1 covers management-approved policies; CISA governance adds zero trust-specific identity maturity metrics and executive sponsorship
